title: Harden _SessionAwareHandler: restore port assertion and strengthen test coverage
analysis: The task-235 refactor of _SessionAwareHandler in dashboard/tests/test_memory.py introduced a regression: lines 67-68 use `if port is not None: self.ports_seen.add(port)` which silently ignores portless URLs. The original inline handlers had `assert port is not None`. This is dangerous because a misconfigured test URL (no port) would bypass both port tracking and fail_port logic, causing tests to pass vacuously. The fix is small and surgical: (1) restore the assertion, (2) add missing negative assertions to two existing tests, (3) add a docstring to __call__. All changes are confined to a single test file. The existing TestSessionAwareHandler class (lines 95-141) already has good structure — we're tightening invariants, not adding new test classes.

steps.description: Write failing test `test_portless_url_raises_assertion_error` in TestSessionAwareHandler that sends a request to a URL without a port (e.g., `http://localhost/mcp`) and asserts it raises AssertionError. This test will fail because the current code silently skips portless URLs.

Restore port assertion in _SessionAwareHandler.__call__ (lines 67-68): replace `if port is not None: self.ports_seen.add(port)` with `assert port is not None, f"Request to {request.url} has no port"` followed by unconditional `self.ports_seen.add(port)`. This makes the failing test pass.

Strengthen test_fail_port_records_port_before_error (line 134): add `assert len(handler.calls) == 0` after the ConnectError context manager to confirm fail_port fires before body parsing populates handler.calls. This assertion should pass immediately since the behavior is already correct.

Strengthen test_fail_port_does_not_affect_other_ports (line 141): add `assert 9000 not in handler.ports_seen` to guard against accidental pre-insertion of the fail_port when only port 9001 was requested. This assertion should pass immediately since the behavior is already correct.

Add docstring to _SessionAwareHandler.__call__ clarifying that `ports_seen` records every attempted port (including failures) while `calls` only records requests that pass all pre-dispatch guards (fail_port check, error_on_all check, and body parsing).

design_decisions.decision: Assert instead of guard clause for port
design_decisions.rationale: Every test URL in this file includes an explicit port (9000, 9001, 8000, 9998, 9999). A portless URL is always a test bug, never a valid scenario. An assertion crash-fails the test with a clear message, while a guard clause would let it pass silently — defeating the purpose of port-tracking logic.

design_decisions.decision: Negative assertions added to existing tests rather than new test methods
design_decisions.rationale: The additional assertions (calls==0, 9000 not in ports_seen) strengthen existing test cases by making their implicit invariants explicit. Creating separate test methods would fragment the logical test scenarios without adding coverage.

design_decisions.decision: Docstring on __call__ rather than class-level update
design_decisions.rationale: The class docstring already describes the handler's purpose. The semantic distinction between ports_seen (all attempts) and calls (successful dispatches) is specific to __call__'s control flow, so documenting it at the method level is more discoverable.

reuse.how: Reuse the existing helper to build portless test requests by constructing with a URL like `http://localhost/mcp` (no port parameter) — the helper just needs a different URL string.
New test method follows the same pattern: create handler, call it, assert. Negative assertions are added inline to existing methods.
